title: Cerastium granulatum
taxonomy: Cerastium granulatum is a species of flowering plant in the family Caryophyllaceae. It is a type of annual or perennial herb that is native to the Mediterranean region. This plant is classified as a member of the genus Cerastium, which includes other species such as the snow-in-summer and mouse-ear chickweed. The species is further classified within the genus based on its characteristics, such as leaf shape and flower color. Cerastium granulatum is a valuable addition to gardens and meadows due to its attractive white or pink flowers and ability to thrive in a variety of environments.
uses: Cerastium granulatum, also known as the granular chickweed, has been used in traditional medicine for its potential anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties. Its leaves and stems are rich in antioxidants and have been traditionally employed to treat various health issues. In herbal medicine, Cerastium granulatum is believed to possess wound-healing properties, making it a valuable remedy for minor cuts and scrapes. Additionally, it has been used to treat digestive issues, such as diarrhea and stomach ulcers, due to its soothing effects on the gastrointestinal tract. Further research is necessary to fully confirm the medicinal uses and benefits of Cerastium granulatum.

properties: Cerastium granulatum, also known as snow-in-summer, is a perennial plant that has been used in traditional medicine for its therapeutic properties. parts: Cerastium granulatum, commonly known as moss campion, has various medicinal parts that have been used in traditional medicine. preparations: Cerastium granulatum is reportedly used in herbal preparations, but there is limited information available on its traditional use and modern applications. intro: Cerastium granulatum, also known as snow-in-summer or granular mouse-eared chickweed, is a perennial herb belonging to the Caryophyllaceae family. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may help to soothe and calm irritated skin and reduce oxidative stress. The bioactive constituents of Cerastium granulatum include flavonoids, such as quercetin and kaempferol, as well as triterpenoid saponins and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Cerastium granulatum is commonly used to prepare topical creams, salves, and infusions, as well as teas and tinctures, to support skin health and alleviate symptoms of inflammation and stress.
